data.title: Federal Reserve extends rate pause for a third straight meeting
data.description:
The Federal Open Market Committee kept its policy rate unchanged at 3.50%-3.75% for a third straight meeting, citing the uncertainty over the U.S. economic and global macro outlooks.

The rate is 75 basis points lower than the 4.25%-4.50% range it was in a year ago.

Developing… Check back for updates.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Artisan Partners expects fixed expenses to rise at low single-digit rate in 2026 while pursuing credit expansion by year-end
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Artisan Partners Asset Management (APAM) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Firm-wide net outflows in the first quarter were $3.1 billion,” Jason Gottlieb said (CEO Jason Gottlieb), adding that “outflows were concentrated in a few equity strategies where we saw clients derisking, reallocating after periods of asset class outperformance and some shifting to passive alternatives.”
  • Gottlieb emphasized offsetting areas of demand, stating, “Year-to-date, we have net inflows in 13 of our investment strategies,” including that “the Sustainable Emerging Market strategy raised $250 million in the first quarter and assets under management are nearing $3 billion,” and “we have continued our multiyear success in growing our credit businesses with $800 million of net inflows in the first quarter… our 15th consecutive quarter of positive credit flows” (CEO Gottlieb).
  • On platform expansion, Gottlieb said, “In the first quarter, we onboarded Grandview Property Partners… and laid the groundwork to launch the team's next flagship fund later this year,” and added, “we… filed an exemptive relief application with the SEC to offer ETF share classes of Artisan mutual funds” (CEO Gottlieb).
  • Charles Daley said (CFO Charles Daley), “Revenues were $303 million, down 10% from the December quarter,” and attributed the sequential change primarily to “the expected absence of performance fees as the December quarter included $29 million of performance fees,” plus “approximately $6 million… due to 2 fewer days in the first quarter of 2026.”

Outlook

  • Daley said (CFO Daley), “Our full year 2026 expense guidance remains unchanged,” and added, “excluding approximately $20 million of incremental fixed expenses related to long-term incentive compensation and Grandview, we continue to expect fixed expenses to increase at low single-digit rate in 2026.”
  • Gottlieb framed near-term business mix expectations, saying, “We expect to see continued strong business development in credit and alternatives, while the backdrop in equities is more challenging and difficult to predict” (CEO Gottlieb).
  • On potential inorganic growth, Gottlieb said, “we think there's a strong possibility that we could get something done by the end of the year” regarding “expand[ing] more traditional credit globally,” while adding, “you never say it's done until it's done” (CEO Gottlieb).

Financial Results

  • Daley said (CFO Daley), “As of March 31, 2026, assets under management were $173 billion, down 4% from the December quarter and up 7% year-over-year,” and added, “Average AUM was $182 billion, up 1% sequentially and up 9% compared to the prior year quarter.”
  • Daley said (CFO Daley), “Adjusted operating expenses increased 4% compared to the December quarter, primarily due to the addition of expenses of Grandview Property Partners, seasonal expenses and the impact of long-term compensation expense.”
  • On capital and seed activity, Daley said (CFO Daley), “Our balance sheet remains strong with $271 million in cash,” and “During the first quarter, we redeemed approximately $50 million of seed capital, reducing seed investments on the balance sheet to $110 million.”
  • On dividends and retained capital, Daley said (CFO Daley), “our Board of Directors declared a quarterly dividend of $0.77 per share… representing a 24% decrease from the prior quarter,” and added, “After funding the quarterly dividend, we retained approximately $150 million of excess capital to support organic growth initiatives, evaluate potential M&A opportunities or return to shareholders.”

Q&A

  • William Katz, TD Cowen: asked where equity reallocation/attrition stands and how the institutional pipeline is shifting; CEO Gottlieb responded that international strategy outflows were largely “rebalancing,” adding, “There hasn't been any termination activity,” while noting “our global opportunity strategy remains a little bit challenged when it comes to some shorter and intermediate-term performance,” alongside positives including “raised net $400 million in flows in the quarter” for a franchise fund and a “very meaningful performance turnaround” in mid-cap growth (CEO Gottlieb).
  • Katz, TD Cowen: asked how the lift-out/M&A pipeline looks versus last quarter and last year; CEO Gottlieb said the pipeline has “incrementally gotten a little bit stronger,” highlighted “a strong possibility” to “get something done… to globalize credit” by year-end, and added that two internal initiatives were “still… in the R&D phase” (CEO Gottlieb).
  • John Dunn, Evercore ISI: asked if APAM is targeting new institutional client segments; CEO Gottlieb said, “I don't think institutionally… there's any new client segment,” but added, “the majority of where we're seeing opportunity is in the intermediate wealth space,” and cited that “the intermediate wealth platform being able to have a slight positive flow for the quarter… is a really good indication,” plus “our second best gross inflow quarter” since early 2021 (CEO Gottlieb).
  • Dunn, Evercore ISI: asked about line of sight to large institutional mandate exits and regional demand; CEO Gottlieb said, “we just don't see any direct line of sight when it comes to massive outflows or massive inflows,” and described “green shoots” where APAM has “good, strong forward lean when it comes to performance,” while acknowledging “inevitably, you're always going to have a strategy or 2 that's got a little bit of a challenge” (CEO Gottlieb).

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts appeared neutral-to-slightly negative, pressing on “equity attrition,” “institutional pipeline,” and “line of sight” to outflows (William Katz, TD Cowen; John Dunn, Evercore ISI).
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more mixed in Q&A, with Gottlieb balancing caution on equities—“more challenging and difficult to predict”—with confidence in credit/alternatives momentum and pipeline activity (CEO Gottlieb).
  • Versus last quarter, the discussion shifted from Grandview launch details to equity outflows and the timing/probability of expanding credit globally, with management again emphasizing pipeline breadth while adding more explicit timing language: “strong possibility… by the end of the year” (CEO Gottlieb).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q1, Daley tied the sequential revenue and dividend decline to the “absence of performance fees,” while Q4 featured “approximately $29 million of performance fees” and a dividend of “$1.01 per share” plus “a $0.57 year-end special dividend” (CFO Daley).
  • Q1 messaging emphasized near-term equity uncertainty—“backdrop in equities is more challenging and difficult to predict”—while Q4 highlighted 2025 results and equity outflows at the platform level, including that “our equity platform was impacted by higher-than-expected outflows of $15.6 billion” (CEO Gottlieb).
  • On strategic development, Q4 positioned Grandview as a newly closed acquisition (“joins Artisan as our 12th autonomous investment franchise”), while Q1 focused on integration and fundraising preparation: “laid the groundwork to launch the team's next flagship fund later this year” (CEO Gottlieb).

Risks and Concerns

  • Gottlieb attributed Q1 outflows to client behavior shifts, including “derisking,” “reallocating,” and “some shifting to passive alternatives,” and flagged strategy-specific pressure: “global opportunity strategy remains a little bit challenged… causing some headwinds” (CEO Gottlieb).
  • Daley highlighted earnings sensitivity to performance fees timing, stating the December quarter included “$29 million of performance fees,” while Q1 reflected “expected absence of performance fees” (CFO Daley).
  • On deal execution, Gottlieb cautioned on closing risk despite optimism: “you never say it's done until it's done” (CEO Gottlieb).

Final Takeaway

Management characterized Q1 as a quarter where equity-driven outflows and the absence of performance fees weighed on results, while credit and alternatives were positioned as the primary engines for business development. Gottlieb pointed to sustained credit inflows, continued alternatives fundraising, distribution expansion into intermediate wealth, and an ETF share-class application, alongside active M&A work that includes a stated “strong possibility” of expanding credit globally “by the end of the year,” with Daley keeping 2026 expense guidance unchanged and reiterating expectations for fixed-expense growth at a low single-digit rate (excluding specified incremental items).

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: SiteOne expects $425M-$455M in 2026 adjusted EBITDA amid 2%-3% pricing and added 53rd-week headwind
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SiteOne Landscape Supply (SITE) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “We are pleased with our first quarter 2026 performance as we overcame the weather and market-related softness in sales volume and delivered 14% adjusted EBITDA growth compared to the prior year period with meaningful gross margin expansion and tight SG&A management,” said Doug Black (President, Chairman of the Board & CEO), while adding that the company acquired “Reinders, a strong fifth-generation market leader in irrigation, agronomics and landscape lighting in the Midwest, which will contribute to our growth this year.”
  • Black said April trends improved but cautioned that “with the recent increase in macroeconomic uncertainty, we believe that our end markets could continue to be soft this year,” while also flagging a pricing tailwind: “we expect pricing to be stronger, which will benefit organic sales growth and gross margin expansion.”
  • Eric Elema (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Secretary) tied Q1 performance to pricing and mix, saying, “Organic daily sales decreased 1% as a result of a 4% decline in volume, partially offset by a 3% increase in pricing,” and added that the company “now expect prices to contribute 2% to 3% to 2026 sales growth.”
  • Daniel Laughlin (Senior VP of Strategy & Development and Executive Officer) detailed the quarter’s deals, including that Reinders “significantly expands our presence in the Midwest and strengthens our capabilities in irrigation and agronomics,” and that “the Reinders leadership team will remain with the business.”
  • Black highlighted a leadership transition in M&A: “Scott Salmon… retired from his role last month,” and “Daniel Laughlin” is “stepping into the role to lead our strategy and acquisition efforts going forward.”

Outlook

  • Black maintained a full-year organic growth stance while updating market assumptions: “Overall, we continue to expect low single-digit growth in organic daily sales for the year,” supported by “2% to 3% growth in pricing,” while warning “end market demand [is] down modestly this year with weakness in new residential construction and repair and upgrade more than offsetting growth in maintenance.”
  • Black said new residential construction “comprises 20% of our sales” and “we expect this market to be down for the full year 2026,” while repair and upgrade “will be down slightly this year due to the increase in macroeconomic uncertainty,” and maintenance “to continue growing steadily in 2026.”
  • Black provided the full-year adjusted EBITDA range: “we expect our full year adjusted EBITDA for fiscal 2026 to be in the range of $425 million to $455 million,” and reiterated that the “extra week will reduce our adjusted EBITDA by $4 million to $5 million,” with the EBITDA range “not factor[ing] in any contribution from unannounced acquisitions.”

Financial Results

  • The company reported Q1 net sales of $940 million (vs. $939 million last year), with Doug Black (President, Chairman of the Board & CEO) attributing the quarter to “a delayed spring season” and Eric Elema (Executive VP, CFO & Assistant Secretary) citing that “winter storms across several regions limited customer activity and delayed applications.”
  • Profitability was framed around margin and cost discipline: Elema said gross profit was $319 million and gross margin was 33.9%, while SG&A was $350 million (37.2% of net sales) and “SG&A in the base business on an adjusted basis was flat.”
  • Bottom-line and cash items included a seasonal loss and higher investing: Elema reported net loss attributable to SiteOne of $26.6 million; cash used in operating activities of $122 million; cash investments of $102 million “primarily” from “the acquisition of Reinders as well as higher capital expenditures”; and capex of $23 million.
  • Balance sheet actions and flexibility were emphasized: Elema reported net debt of $585 million and net debt to trailing 12-month adjusted EBITDA of 1.4x; available liquidity of $502 million; and said, “Post quarter end, we amended our ABL facility and extended the maturity date to April 2031.”

Q&A

  • David Manthey, Baird: Asked about “the long-term margin improvement opportunity… next 3 to 5 years” and key 2026 initiatives; Doug Black (President, Chairman of the Board & CEO) replied, “we have our path to 13%,” citing private label, small-customer penetration, focus-branch improvement, and “delivery efficiency.”
  • Manthey, Baird: Asked private label mix and growth drivers; Black clarified “the 40% were our high-growth private label product lines,” said total private label “grew at 10%,” added “We’re approximately 15% private label,” and said the company is “looking to increase that by 100 basis points a year,” with an “ultimately… 25%, 30%” goal.
  • Ryan Merkel, William Blair: Asked to separate weather vs. macro impacts and referenced, “You missed the Street by about $40 million”; Black said it’s “hard to discern,” pointed to maintenance seasonality, and said the company “dropped our guide for the market… now we’re saying it’s going to be modestly down.”
  • Merkel, William Blair: Asked about price cadence and assumptions for PVC/fertilizer; Elema said Q1 was 3%, Q2 “a little firmer 3,” and “2% maybe in the second half,” calling 2%-3% “a fairly conservative point right now.”
  • Michael Dahl, RBC: Asked about EBITDA bridge between gross margin vs. SG&A leverage; Elema said leverage is expected “in Q2 and Q3,” while the mix has “shifted up in favor of gross margin” versus an earlier “50-50 contribution.”
  • Dahl, RBC: Asked about incremental cost actions; Black said management will “take action to manage labor tightly,” while Elema noted fuel costs flowing through SG&A and a fuel surcharge that “is in sales.”
  • Keith Hughes, Truist: Asked if price increases are sticking; Black said, “we’ve been able to pass through price increases.” Hughes also asked on timing; Elema said price moves are “pretty much concurrently.”
  • Matthew Bouley, Barclays: Asked to quantify how much gross margin expansion is structural vs. inflationary; management said it does “typically don’t give a breakdown specific by initiative.” Bouley also asked about Reinders margins; Black said Reinders should be “nice and profitable this year… probably around where we are,” with “significant upside” as synergies build.
  • Jeffrey Stevenson, Loop: Asked if fertilizer/PVC inflation could defer maintenance demand; Black said fertilizer’s “increase around 5%… will [not] create any kind of demand degradation,” and said SiteOne doesn’t “anticipate… any shortages.” Stevenson also asked new-residential magnitude; Black said “down mid- to high single digits.”
  • Charles Perron-Piché, Goldman Sachs: Asked if customers are leaning into digital/delivery and about tech advantage; Black cited “digital sales are up 60%” and said “about 1/3 of our business is delivered.” Perron-Piché also asked on buybacks vs. M&A; Elema said repurchases were “opportunistic,” with “first focused on growth, M&A,” and Black said, “the pipeline is healthy.”
  • Shaun Calnan, BofA: Asked to quantify April volume improvement and Q2 growth; Black said April volumes “have improved” but “aren’t positive,” and said quarterly seasonality makes it “really hard to call volume growth by quarter.” Calnan also asked about pre-buying ahead of price increases; Black said that “tends to happen,” but customers “don’t have massive storage.”
  • Collin Verron, Deutsche Bank: Asked about COGS/inventory cost dynamics and freight handling/distribution; Elema said it was “private label… product mix” and being “fully stocked,” and flagged freight drivers including “rising cost of diesel,” “international freight,” and “our fifth DC.”
  • Matthew Johnson, UBS: Asked about fertilizer cost exposure and inventory; Black said urea is up but is “only one component,” and reiterated the “5%… passing through costs,” while Elema said supply looks good “into the fall.” Johnson also asked focus-branch improvement; Black said, “we had improvement… in the first quarter,” and “we can move the needle even in the softer market.”
  • Andrew Carter, Stifel: Asked if Reinders could contribute “$8 million or $9 million” of EBITDA; Black replied, “Yes, you kind of nailed it… that helps us have confidence in our range.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly negative to neutral, with pointed framing on the revenue gap (“You missed the Street by about $40 million”) and repeated probing on the durability of pricing and the sources of margin expansion.
  • Management sentiment was slightly positive in prepared remarks (emphasizing “meaningful gross margin expansion,” “tight SG&A management,” and acquisition momentum) and more cautious in Q&A when discussing macro uncertainty, with hedged language such as “hard to discern,” “some uncertainty,” and “we believe.”
  • Compared with Q4 2025, management introduced more explicit near-term caution (weather disruption, “recent increase in macroeconomic uncertainty,” and end markets “down modestly”), while continuing to stress operational levers (private label, delivery efficiency, focus branches) that were also central themes last quarter.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance framework was reiterated: in both calls, Black guided 2026 adjusted EBITDA to $425 million to $455 million and repeated that the 53rd week “will reduce our adjusted EBITDA by $4 million to $5 million.”
  • Pricing outlook tightened upward within the prior range: Q4 described 2026 pricing as “up 1% to 3%,” while Q1 commentary said, “we now expect to achieve 2% to 3% growth in pricing.”
  • Market demand language became more cautious: Q4 said “end market demand [to] be flat,” with repair and upgrade “flat in 2026,” while Q1 shifted to “end market demand… down modestly” and repair and upgrade “down slightly this year.”
  • Acquisition leadership changed: Q4 featured Scott Salmon (Executive Vice President of Strategy & Development) leading M&A discussion, while Q1 highlighted his retirement and Daniel Laughlin taking over.

Risks and Concerns

  • Black said “macroeconomic uncertainty,” “higher interest rates,” “energy volatility,” and “lower consumer confidence” are pressuring new residential construction and repair and upgrade demand.
  • Elema cited ongoing deflation in grass seed and PVC (down 10% and 8% in Q1), alongside higher “freight and distribution costs,” and fuel-cost volatility in delivery, with mitigation including fuel surcharges and efficiency initiatives.
  • Black flagged the calendar impact of the “extra week” in a “traditionally loss-making period,” which management expects to be a $4 million to $5 million adjusted EBITDA headwind.

Final Takeaway

Management described a Q1 shaped by weather-driven volume pressure and rising macro uncertainty, while emphasizing pricing strength, gross margin expansion driven by commercial initiatives (including private label and small customers), and continued cost discipline. SiteOne reiterated full-year 2026 adjusted EBITDA guidance of $425 million to $455 million (including the $4 million to $5 million 53rd-week headwind) and positioned acquisitions such as Reinders, alongside a still-robust pipeline, as incremental support while end markets are expected to be modestly down.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Oklo leads all large-cap utilities stocks in YoY CapEx growth
data.description:
Capital expenditure growth is a key indicator of how aggressively a company is investing in its future, whether through new infrastructure, equipment, technology, or capacity expansion.

The following list ranks U.S. utilities stocks by their year-over-year CapEx growth percentage, offering a data-driven look at which companies are putting the most capital to work relative to the prior year.

Below is a list of 10 U.S. utilities stocks ranked according to their year-over-year capital expenditure growth percentage. The list spans multiple sub-sectors within utilities, including Electric Utilities, Multi-Utilities, Gas Utilities, and Water Utilities.

Oklo (OKLO) tops the list by an enormous margin with a CapEx growth rate of 9,333.24% year-over-year, investing $33.2 million in fiscal 2025 capital expenditures compared to just $352,000 in the prior year, a near-zero base effect that reflects the advanced nuclear company's shift from pure R&D into active site preparation and infrastructure buildout as it works toward commercializing its Aurora microreactor technology.

NRG Energy (NRG) and WEC Energy Group (WEC) follow in a more conventional second and third place with growth rates of 143.01% and 58.14% respectively, while Xcel Energy (XEL) and MDU Resources (MDU) round out the top five at 48.13% and 47.35%.

American Electric Power (AEP) and MGE Energy (MGEE) occupy the sixth and seventh positions with CapEx growth of 47.32% and 44.86%, while PPL Corporation (PPL) and The Southern Company (SO) follow at 43.67% and 42.23%.

H2O America (HTO) rounds out the list at 38.69%, having dramatically expanded its five-year CapEx plan by 31% to $2.7 billion through 2030, driven by accelerated pipeline replacement, PFAS water treatment investments, and Texas expansion, with an expected 13% compound annual rate base growth through the period.

American Electric Power (AEP) stands as the sole Buy at 4.27, while MDU Resources (MDU) and MGE Energy (MGEE) both carry Sell ratings at 2.36 and 2.39 respectively, highlighting a rare split where the best-rated stock in a regulated sector is actively growing capital spend while two other heavy spenders are flagged as deteriorating by the quant model.

Here is the list:

  1. Oklo Inc. (OKLO), CapEx growth YoY: 9,333.24%

  2. NRG Energy, Inc. (NRG), CapEx growth YoY: 143.01%

  3. WEC Energy Group, Inc. (WEC), CapEx growth YoY: 58.14%

  4. Xcel Energy Inc. (XEL), CapEx growth YoY: 48.13%

  5. MDU Resources Group, Inc. (MDU), CapEx growth YoY: 47.35%

  6. American Electric Power Company, Inc. (AEP), CapEx growth YoY: 47.32%

  7. MGE Energy, Inc. (MGEE), CapEx growth YoY: 44.86%

  8. PPL Corporation (PPL), CapEx growth YoY: 43.67%

  9. The Southern Company (SO), CapEx growth YoY: 42.23%

  10. H2O America (HTO), CapEx growth YoY: 38.69%

Utilities ETFs: (XLU), (VPU), (FUTY), (IDU), (FXU), and (JXI)

data.title: Twilio Q1 2026 Earnings Preview: Cautious views ahead of results
data.description:
Twilio (TWLO) is scheduled to announce Q1 earnings results on Thursday, April 30th, after market close.

The consensus EPS Estimate is $1.27 (+11.4% Y/Y), and the consensus revenue estimate is $1.34B (+14.5% Y/Y).

Analysts were mixed on Twilio’s (TWLO) outlook for 2026 after its Q4 results, even as the communications software company’s guidance was above Wall Street's forecast.

For Q1, Twilio said at the time it expects sales of $1.335B-$1.345B, and adjusted earnings are forecast to be between $1.21 and $1.26.

RBC Capital said that the 2026 guidance "underwhelms," and its analysts said at the time that they "remain cautious around long-term margin profiles and the potential for accelerating organic topline growth."

A recent analysis by SA contributor Tyler Wiedwald said that the communications software company is "transitioning from high-growth disruptor to mature platform with moderate growth and increasing profit margins."

"Margin pressure persists from carrier A2P messaging fees, while high-margin software and AI products remain too small to materially impact consolidated margins," Wiedwald said.

Over the last 2 years, TWLO has beaten EPS estimates 88% of the time and has beaten revenue estimates 100% of the time.

Over the last 3 months, EPS estimates have seen 14 upward revisions and 5 downward. Revenue estimates have seen 21 upward revisions and 1 downward.

data.title: CoreWeave building out capacity faster-than-expected, Oppenheimer says
data.description:
• Based on the latest checks, CoreWeave (CRWV) appears to be building out data center capacity faster-than-expected, which could drive revenue upside this year, investment firm Oppenheimer said.
  • “CoreWeave reports 1Q earnings on May 7th, and we expect revenue at the high end of guidance and for management to raise 2026 revenue materially above $12-13 billion,” analyst Param Singh wrote in a note to clients. “This is based on our industry checks that indicate CoreWeave is building out capacity faster than anticipated and can add another 1GW by the September quarter (above guidance/expectations). We also expect the industrywide shortage of AI data center capacity to persist for multiple years and anticipate that CoreWeave can grow to at least 6GW during that time.”
  • Delving deeper, Singh said CoreWeave is expected to bring online 1GW of additional capacity by the third quarter, which is higher than the guidance for the full year. As such, that should translate to potential revenue upside for this year as well, Singh added.
  • Singh has an Outperform rating and a $150 price target on CoreWeave.

data.title: HB Wealth's Adams warns: Only the biggest growth stocks are actually recovering
data.description:
Large-cap growth stocks have staged an eye-catching comeback from their late-March lows, with investors rotating back into perennial favorites on the assumption that recovery from war-time economic strain will follow a similar path to the rebound seen during peak tariff-policy uncertainty.

But according to Gina Martin Adams, Chief Market Strategist for HB Wealth, the strength beneath the surface may be more illusion than reality.

The key problem, Adams argues, is breadth, or more precisely, the lack of it. The gains across large-cap growth have been almost entirely captured by the very biggest names, while the average growth stock remains in negative territory for the year.

The divergence is starkest when comparing the S&P 500 Pure Growth and Russell 1000 Pure Growth indices. More than 60% of stocks in the S&P 500 version are trading back above their 200-day moving average, yet only 32% of growth stocks in the broader Russell 1000 have managed the same. "What appears to be a strong surge in growth stocks," Adams notes, "is actually quite a narrow recovery in the largest growth stocks among U.S. large caps."

The duration of this breadth weakness is where the signal becomes harder to ignore, according to Adams. Fewer than half of Russell 1000 Pure Growth stocks have been above their 200-day moving average since January 8th, the longest such streak since 2022, and a pattern Adams identifies as consistent with a bear market in the growth style. She points to the corrections of 2018 and 2022 as the only comparable periods, both of which saw value stocks outperform and growth fall into a sustained bear market.

Value, Adams argues, is doing what growth cannot right now: delivering broad participation. While the largest value stocks have lagged the wider gains seen across the rest of the value universe, that dynamic reflects a healthier and more evenly distributed rally, strength spread across the style rather than concentrated in a handful of mega-caps.

 In Adams' view, a growth rally held up by only its heaviest constituents is a fragile one, and history suggests the cracks beneath the surface tend to widen before they close.

data.title: Idex forecasts 2026 adjusted EPS of $8.35-$8.55 as it lifts organic growth outlook to 3%-4%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: IDEX Corporation (IEX) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "IDEX delivered a strong first quarter" with "organic sales growth of 5% and adjusted EBITDA margin of 26%" and "orders were better than expected, growing 10% organically year-over-year" (CEO, President & Director Eric Ashleman).

  • "Strength was most pronounced in our Health & Science Technologies" and "the strong backlog build in HST improves our visibility to deliver continued solid growth for the balance of the year and into 2027" (CEO, President & Director Ashleman).

  • "We repurchased $76 million of IDEX shares in the first quarter and expect to maintain that pace throughout 2026" (CEO, President & Director Ashleman).

  • "Adjusted gross margin declined 40 basis points year-over-year to 44.9%" and "free cash flow of $86 million declined $5 million versus last year, driven mostly by higher working capital investment due to higher growth" (Senior VP & CFO Sean Gillen).

Outlook

  • "For the second quarter of 2026, we expect ... adjusted EPS of $2.07 to $2.12" (Senior VP & CFO Gillen) vs. $2.09 (analysts’ estimate).

  • "For the full year 2026, we now expect organic growth in the 3% to 4% range" and "we are increasing our adjusted EPS guidance for 2026 by $0.20 to $8.35 to $8.55" (Senior VP & CFO Gillen).

  • "Adjusted EBITDA margin is expected to be in the 26.5% to 27% range in 2026, unchanged from our previous guidance" with headwinds as "volume decrementals in FMT and FSDP and mix influences keep our near-term margin expansion expectations unchanged" (Senior VP & CFO Gillen).

  • On tariffs, "the IEEPA tariffs have been repealed" but "the administration has implemented new tariffs" and "we currently do not anticipate much of a net impact to our financial results"; the company has applied for refunds: "we have taken the requisite actions to apply for these" (Senior VP & CFO Gillen).

Financial results

  • "Organic revenue growth of 5%" and "adjusted EBITDA margin expanded 50 basis points year-over-year" while "adjusted EPS came in significantly higher than our guided range in the first quarter" (Senior VP & CFO Gillen).

  • Segment demand and profitability were framed as "HST's organic order growth of 17% year-over-year" with "HST adjusted EBITDA margin expanded 100 basis points"; "FMT orders grew 9%" with "FMT's adjusted EBITDA margin declined slightly by 10 basis points"; and "FSDP orders declined 4%" with "FSDP adjusted EBITDA margin increased 30 basis points" (Senior VP & CFO Gillen).

  • Capital and cash were highlighted as "we ended the quarter with strong liquidity of approximately $1.1 billion" and leverage "at roughly 2x" (Senior VP & CFO Gillen).

Q&A

  • Joseph Giordano, TD Cowen: Asked why "the second half organic decelerate" if orders are strong; Senior VP & CFO Gillen said HST should stay strong, but FMT is modeled flatter due to "some uncertainty in the macro world" and limited visibility.

  • Matt Summerville, D.A. Davidson: Asked about life sciences inflection and order cadence; CEO, President & Director Ashleman said life sciences is "about exactly where we thought it would be" with "pharma, really, really strong" but pressure from "the China market" and "NIH funding"; he added FMT/FSDP cadence was "pretty soft" in January, "came back" in February, "much stronger" in March, and "stayed at that level" in April.

  • Nathan Jones, Stifel: Pressed on short-cycle industrial lag and HST incremental margins; CEO, President & Director Ashleman pointed to areas "indexed to chemical markets" and "core energy" as softer; Senior VP & CFO Gillen said moving toward 40% HST incrementals depends on lifting margins in acquired businesses via "8020 actions" and improved life sciences.

  • Deane Dray, RBC: Asked about water strength and M&A implications; CEO, President & Director Ashleman said municipal inspection/analytics and equipment were strong and high-purity water for semicon "flipped over" to growth; on sector deals, he said it is "a testament to the quality of the work that we do."

  • Michael Halloran, Baird: Asked if the guide uplift is cyclical; CEO, President & Director Ashleman replied, "That's absolutely true" that the change is tied to "internal growth initiatives" more than a cyclical turn; Senior VP & CFO Gillen added M&A pipeline is "active" and bolt-on focused while maintaining buyback cadence.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, repeatedly probing why short-cycle industrial is not inflecting and why HST margins/flow-through are below prior levels, including questions like "what needs to happen" to get margins back to "30-ish percent" (Joseph Giordano, TD Cowen).

  • Management tone was slightly positive and confident on HST visibility and capital return, while guarded on industrial cyclicality, describing conditions as "mixed" and tied to "uncertainty of geopolitical stuff" (CEO, President & Director Ashleman).

  • Versus the prior quarter, management language shifted from "we are not seeing an inflection point" (Q4) to describing "encouraging positive signs of early inflection" (Q1), while still emphasizing conservatism due to macro/geopolitical uncertainty.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The biggest change was raised 2026 organic growth guidance to "3% to 4%" (Q1) from "1% to 2%" (Q4) and raised adjusted EPS guidance to "$8.35 to $8.55" (Q1) from "$8.15 to $8.35" (Q4), while keeping the adjusted EBITDA margin range unchanged at "26.5% to 27%" (both quarters) (Senior VP & CFO Gillen).

  • HST momentum moderated from "organic orders growth of 34%" in Q4 to "17%" in Q1, with Q1 commentary emphasizing backlog-driven visibility "into 2027" rather than Q4’s focus on a "record backlog" and year-end order acceleration (CEO, President & Director Ashleman; Senior VP & CFO Gillen).

  • Industrial commentary moved from Q4’s "have not seen them inflect up yet" on short-cycle diagnostics to Q1’s month-by-month improvement pattern, but still described as "appropriately conservatively modeled" given geopolitical overhang (CEO, President & Director Ashleman).

Risks and concerns

  • Life sciences demand headwinds were tied to "the China market" and "NIH funding" pressures; management positioned mitigation as "focus on what we do best" through customer innovation and using IDEX’s "great global scale" to support shifting footprints (CEO, President & Director Ashleman).

  • Short-cycle end-market risk remained centered on chemicals and energy-linked exposures and uneven diagnostics, with management characterizing conditions as "mixed" and tied to "macro" and "geopolitical" uncertainty (CEO, President & Director Ashleman).

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 as a better-than-expected start with 5% organic revenue growth, 10% organic order growth, and a backlog-led visibility improvement concentrated in HST, supporting a higher 2026 outlook for organic growth (3%-4%) and adjusted EPS ($8.35-$8.55) while keeping the full-year adjusted EBITDA margin range unchanged at 26.5%-27%. The company reiterated plans to sustain $76 million of quarterly share repurchases in 2026 and framed near-term caution primarily around macro/geopolitical uncertainty in FMT and FSDP, with ongoing 8020 actions and acquired-business margin work positioned as key levers to improve HST profitability over time.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: ICON rebounds as accounting probe concludes
data.description:
Despite a ~36% YTD drop, shares of ICON Public Limited (ICLR) bounced back on Wednesday, adding ~13% after the contract research organization announced the conclusion of a previously disclosed investigation into its accounting practices.

Icon (ICLR) shares plunged in early February after the Irish firm pulled its full-year outlook, projecting “material weaknesses” due to an ongoing investigation into the design and operation of certain internal controls related to financial reporting.

In a statement on Wednesday, the company disclosed that its audit committee has concluded the investigation, noting some overstatement of its revenue figures for 2023 and 2024, but lower than the previously disclosed upper limit of 2%.

The committee identified similar issues in relation to ICLR’s 2025 financials, but the impact was lower than in the prior years. As a result, the company plans to restate results for 2023, 2024, and the first nine months of the year.

Icon (ICLR) added that its financial statements and the external audit process will be complete in the coming weeks, after which it plans to submit its Annual Report on Form 20-F and issue a more comprehensive business update.

“I am also pleased with execution on our commercial strategy,” CEO Barry Balfe said, adding, “While near-term performance will continue to be impacted by recent market dynamics, we are encouraged by the improved demand environment.”

data.title: T-Mobile raised to Outperform on Q1 results, free cash flow, and M&A opportunities
data.description:
Following Q1 results, T-Mobile (TMUS) earned an upgrade at Oppenheimer, which now views the stock as a buy with an ambitious price target that represents 40% upside to Tuesday’s closing price.

After the close on Tuesday, the company reported an 11% increase in revenue for Q1, exceeding expectations thanks to postpaid services, which realized a 15% increase in revenue to $15.6B. The company also raised its full-year postpaid guidance to 950K to 1.05M net account additions and core adjusted EBITDA to a range of $37.1B and $37.5B.

In reviewing the results, Oppenheimer analyst Timothy Horan expects T-Mobile (TMUS) to focus on closing the 20% price discount with peers while maintaining cost controls to keep expenses flat year-over-year, largely by relying more heavily on artificial intelligence.

Increases to its free cash flow to an “industry-leading 13% per year” on strong margins and buybacks also support Horan’s upgrade to Outperform.

The recent joint ventures present "an equity value creation opportunity rather than part of the convergence proposition,” Horan says, anticipating a continued pursuit of fiber acquisitions under similar structures. 

The bottom line? T-Mobile retains major advantages in spectrum, AI/digitalization, and costs. “Long-term guidance is also likely conservative with room for upside from M&A and synergies,” Horan says, adding that the current 11% free cash flow yield and 5-year low of ~8x EV/EBITDA create “an attractive entry point.”

T-Mobile shares are up 7% on Wednesday.
